title: Boston police seeking suspect in armed robbery
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Boston police seek public's help to identify a suspect in a January 22 armed robbery in Dorchester.
The suspect allegedly robbed multiple victims at gunpoint around 4:30 p.m. at Adams and Whitten Streets.
Information can be provided to detectives at 617-343-4335 or anonymously via the CrimeStoppers tip line.

title: No license yet for World Cup games as Foxborough asks for public safety funding up front
author: Molly Farrar
bullets: Foxborough officials demand $7.8 million in public safety funding from World Cup host committee before issuing a license.
Boston Soccer 2026 proposes a post-event payment process, backed by Kraft Sports, to cover public safety costs.
Massachusetts awaits over $46 million in federal security funds; Governor Healey confident games will occur as scheduled.

title: Wu to host first-ever ‘office hours’ for residents to speak with her directly
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Mayor Michelle Wu will hold a first-ever 'office hours' event for Boston residents on Thursday at the Bruce C. Bolling Building.
The event allows residents to speak directly with Wu and city department staff, including those from schools, police, and housing.
City services like census registration and printing certificates will be offered during the two-hour session in Roxbury.

title: 7 players Bruins could target at 2026 NHL trade deadline
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: The Boston Bruins are considering trading prospects and draft picks for long-term players at the 2026 NHL trade deadline.
Potential trade targets include Robert Thomas from St. Louis, Conor Garland from Vancouver, and Rasmus Ristolainen from Philadelphia.
Focus is on adding impactful talent like Justin Faulk or Simon Nemec rather than short-term rentals to remain postseason contenders.

title: Staff member at special needs residential program in Franklin accused of breaking autistic student’s arm
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Saturs Allison, 27, of Pawtucket, R.I., is accused of assaulting and injuring an autistic student in Franklin, Massachusetts.
Court records show Allison allegedly violated Amego, Inc.'s restraint policies by physically restraining the student, leading to a broken arm.
Allison has been suspended from his role, pleaded not guilty, and faces up to five years in prison if convicted.

title: ‘Every run is about defying cancer’: This 18-year-old is running Boston for her childhood friend
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Amanda Lewis, 18, from Charlotte, North Carolina, is running the Boston Marathon to honor her late friend Lily.
Lewis is participating with the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute to raise funds for cancer research and awareness.
Her marathon journey began after losing Lily to brain cancer, using running as a way to cope and make a difference.

title: Fall River man sentenced for raping woman when she refused to take naked pictures of young daughter
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Elias Rodrigues Dos Santos Mendes, 55, sentenced to 8-10 years for rape and child pornography charges in Fall River.
Dos Santos Mendes asked a woman to take nude photos of her daughter and raped her when she refused.
After serving his sentence, undocumented Dos Santos Mendes will be deported to Brazil, according to Bristol County DA.

title: What’s next for Patriots’ offense after releasing Stefon Diggs?
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: The Patriots released Stefon Diggs, freeing up $17 million in cap space for potential roster upgrades.
New England may pursue free-agent wide receiver Alec Pierce or trade for A.J. Brown to replace Diggs.
Additional options for replacing Diggs include drafting a receiver with the 31st overall pick in the 2026 NFL Draft.

title: Each mile is for her miracle: This Granby mom is running the Boston Marathon with her daughter in mind
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Brianna Poehler from Granby, Mass. will run the 2026 Boston Marathon to support Boston Children's Hospital.
Poehler's daughter, a liver transplant survivor, received life-saving care at Boston Children's Hospital as an infant.
She aims to raise funds for research and care by participating with the Miles for Miracles team.

title: Do you support the U.S. strikes on Iran? Here’s what 300 readers said.
author: Autumn Sloboda
bullets: 54% of over 350 Boston.com readers support U.S. strikes on Iran; 43% oppose; 3% are unsure.
Supporters cite Iran's involvement in regional conflicts and describe its government as a 'brutal regime.'
Opponents fear escalation into broader war and emphasize the lack of congressional approval for strikes.
